annotations.label: Turing machine

anchor.snippet: A Turing machine is a mathematical model of computation describing an abstract machine

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the (Post-)Turing machine as a function `Λ → Γ → Option (Λ × Stmt)` operating on a `Tape`, the standard equivalent of Turing's one-tape model.

annotations.label: Turing completeness

anchor.snippet: Turing completeness is the ability for a model of computation or a system of instructions to simulate a Turing machine

annotations.note: Mathlib proves specific model simulations but has no general definition of Turing completeness.

annotations.label: Recursively enumerable language

anchor.snippet: A set of strings which can be enumerated in this manner is called a recursively enumerable language

annotations.note: `REPred` defines recursively enumerable predicates over any `Primcodable` type (subsuming languages of strings) as domains of partial recursive functions.

annotations.label: Halting problem is unsolvable

anchor.snippet: This is due to the fact that the halting problem is unsolvable

annotations.note: `ComputablePred.halting_problem` proves the predicate `fun c => (eval c n).Dom` is not computable.

annotations.label: Universal Turing machine

anchor.snippet: A Turing machine that is able to simulate any other Turing machine is called a universal Turing machine

annotations.note: Mathlib provides a universal partial recursive function `eval` (a code interpreter) rather than an actual universal Turing machine.

annotations.label: Church–Turing thesis

anchor.snippet: This thesis states that Turing machines, lambda calculus, and other similar formalisms of computation do indeed capture the informal notion of effective methods

annotations.note: The Church–Turing thesis is an informal claim and is not (and cannot be) a formal theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Turing machine components

anchor.snippet: More explicitly, a Turing machine consists of

annotations.note: The tape, head, state register, and instruction table are formalized via `Turing.Tape`, `Turing.TM0.Cfg` (label + tape), and `Turing.TM0.Machine`.

annotations.label: Formal 7-tuple definition

anchor.snippet: a (one-tape) Turing machine can be formally defined as a 7- tuple

annotations.note: Mathlib encodes the machine as a single transition function rather than the explicit 7-tuple (Q,Γ,b,Σ,δ,q₀,F) with accept states.

annotations.label: Accepted tape contents

anchor.snippet: The initial tape contents is said to be accepted by

annotations.note: `Turing.TM0.eval` returns a `Part` whose domain captures halting, but Mathlib's TM0 has no accept/reject-state notion of acceptance.

annotations.label: 3-state busy beaver 7-tuple

anchor.snippet: The 7-tuple for the 3-state busy beaver looks like this

annotations.note: No busy beaver machine is constructed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fixed-length tape reduces to LBA or FSM

anchor.section: Additional details required to visualise or implement Turing machines
anchor.snippet: would seriously limit the range of computations the machine can perform to those of a linear bounded automaton

annotations.note: Linear bounded automata are not defined in Mathlib and this reduction is not stated.

annotations.label: Adding no-shift does not increase power

anchor.snippet: This would not increase the machine's computational power

annotations.note: No stay/no-shift variant or equivalence proof exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 5-tuple convention (definition 1)

anchor.snippet: The most common convention represents each

annotations.note: Mathlib's transition function uses separate `move`/`write` statements rather than the combined 5-tuple instruction convention.

annotations.label: 5-tuple convention (definition 2)

anchor.snippet: adopt a different convention, with new state

annotations.note: This alternative 5-tuple convention is not directly used; Mathlib's `TM0.Stmt` is `move` or `write`.

annotations.label: Any Turing table from nine 5-tuples

anchor.snippet: Any Turing table (list of instructions) can be constructed from the above nine 5-tuples

annotations.note: This construction over a fixed instruction set is not formalized.

annotations.label: State formula

anchor.snippet: includes both the current instruction and all the symbols on the tape

annotations.note: `Turing.TM0.Cfg` bundles the current label and full tape, capturing the concept though not Turing's specific 'state formula' notation.

annotations.label: Complete configuration

anchor.snippet: He calls it "the complete configuration

annotations.note: `Turing.TM0.Cfg` is exactly the complete configuration: the machine state together with its tape.

annotations.label: Instantaneous description

anchor.snippet: Hopcroft and Ullman call this composite the

annotations.note: Mathlib's docstring describes `Cfg` as the 'instantaneous configurations', matching the instantaneous description.

annotations.label: Total state of busy beaver after 3 moves

anchor.snippet: Example: total state of 3-state 2-symbol busy beaver after 3

annotations.note: No busy beaver machine or its trace is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivalent models have no more power than UTM

anchor.snippet: Many machines that might be thought to have more computational capability than a simple universal Turing machine can be shown to have no more power

annotations.note: Mathlib proves concrete model equivalences (e.g. `TM1to0.tr_eval`, TM2↔partrec) but not the general 'no more powerful' meta-statement.

annotations.label: TM equivalent to relaxed single-stack PDA

anchor.snippet: A Turing machine is equivalent to a single-stack pushdown automaton (PDA) that has been made more flexible and concise by relaxing the last-in-first-out (LIFO) requirement of its stack

annotations.note: Pushdown automata and this equivalence are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: TM equivalent to two-stack PDA

anchor.snippet: a Turing machine is also equivalent to a two-stack PDA with standard LIFO semantics

annotations.note: Mathlib's TM2 is a multi-stack machine model proven Turing-equivalent (via ToPartrec), but the explicit two-stack PDA equivalence is not stated.

annotations.label: Turing-equivalent

anchor.snippet: some very simple models turn out to be Turing-equivalent

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named definition of Turing equivalence, only individual simulation theorems.

annotations.label: Read-only right-moving TMs equivalent to DFAs

anchor.snippet: Read-only, right-moving Turing machines are equivalent to DFAs

annotations.note: DFAs are formalized (`Mathlib.Computability.DFA`) but read-only TMs and this equivalence are not.

annotations.label: Some programming languages Turing complete, others not

anchor.snippet: have shown that among the general-purpose programming languages some are Turing complete while others are not

annotations.note: Turing completeness of programming languages is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: ANSI C is not Turing complete

anchor.snippet: ANSI C is not Turing complete, as all instantiations of ANSI C

annotations.note: No statement about ANSI C exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Automatic machine vs choice machine

anchor.snippet: Turing makes a distinction between an

annotations.note: Mathlib's Turing machines are deterministic by construction; no choice/c-machine variant is defined.

annotations.label: Oracle machine (o-machine)

anchor.snippet: An oracle machine or o-machine is a Turing a-machine that pauses its computation at state

annotations.note: `Nat.RecursiveIn O f` formalizes oracle (relative) computability recursion-theoretically rather than as a TM with an oracle tape.

annotations.label: Deterministic TM mimics nondeterministic TM

anchor.snippet: This is indeed the technique by which a deterministic

annotations.note: Nondeterministic Turing machines are not defined in Mathlib, so this simulation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Existence of universal machine

anchor.snippet: It is possible to invent a single machine which can be used to compute any computable sequence

annotations.note: `exists_code` shows `eval` is a universal partial recursive function, the recursion-theoretic analogue of Turing's universal machine.

annotations.label: Logarithmic slowdown of universal multitape TM

anchor.snippet: a multi-tape universal Turing machine need only be slower by logarithmic factor compared to the machines it simulates

annotations.note: No complexity bound on universal multitape simulation is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: TMs more powerful than FSMs and PDAs

anchor.snippet: Turing machines are more powerful than some other kinds of automata, such as finite-state machines and pushdown automata

annotations.note: Mathlib defines DFAs/NFAs but does not prove a power hierarchy relating them to Turing machines.

annotations.label: TM computes anything a real computer can

anchor.snippet: Anything a real computer can compute, a Turing machine can also compute

annotations.note: No model of a real computer or such a comparison is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: RASP machine model

anchor.snippet: the random-access stored-program machine or RASP machine model

annotations.note: The RASP / random-access machine model is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Binary search faster in RASP model

anchor.snippet: An example of this is binary search

annotations.note: No complexity comparison of binary search across machine models is formalized.

annotations.label: Arithmetic model vs Turing model

anchor.section: Comparison with the arithmetic model of computation
anchor.snippet: The arithmetic model of computation differs from the Turing model in two aspects

annotations.note: The arithmetic model of computation is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclidean algorithm polynomial in Turing model only

anchor.section: Comparison with the arithmetic model of computation
anchor.snippet: The Euclidean algorithm runs in polynomial time in the Turing model, but not in the arithmetic model

annotations.note: No bit-complexity analysis of the Euclidean algorithm across models is formalized.

annotations.label: Strongly polynomial time

anchor.section: Comparison with the arithmetic model of computation
anchor.snippet: Such an algorithm is said to run in strongly polynomial time

annotations.note: Strongly polynomial time is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Entscheidungsproblem

anchor.section: The Entscheidungsproblem (the "decision problem"): Hilbert's tenth question of 1900
anchor.snippet: is solved when we know a procedure that allows for any given logical expression to decide by finitely many operations its validity or satisfiability

annotations.note: The decision problem for first-order validity is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gandy's five operations of the analytical engine

anchor.snippet: Gandy's analysis of Babbage's analytical engine describes the following five operations

annotations.note: Gandy's operations of the analytical engine are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Operations (1),(2),(4) yield Turing-computable functions

anchor.snippet: the functions which can be calculated by (1), (2), and (4) are precisely those which are Turing computable

annotations.note: This characterization in terms of Gandy's operations is not formalized.

annotations.label: Computable function (Turing 1939)

anchor.snippet: The development of these ideas leads to the author's definition of a computable function, and to an identification of computability with effective calculability

annotations.note: Mathlib defines computability via partial recursive functions (`Computable`/`Partrec`), the standard formalization identified with effective calculability, rather than via Turing machines directly.

annotations.label: Entscheidungsproblem has no solution

anchor.snippet: the Hilbert Entscheidungsproblem can have no solution

annotations.note: The undecidability of the Entscheidungsproblem is not formalized; Mathlib only has the (related) halting problem result.

annotations.label: Turing's second proof: does machine print 0

anchor.snippet: Turing's example (his second proof)

annotations.note: Turing's specific 'prints 0' undecidability argument is not formalized in Mathlib.
